step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("tooth eruption delay management options age 10 months")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

Based on the retrieved information, the majority of the evidence points towards delayed tooth eruption being common enough to consider a wait-and-watch approach unless accompanied by other concerning symptoms or underlying conditions. Given the variability seen even within "normal" parameters for tooth eruption in young children, the current situation does not inherently call for extensive diagnostics like OPG scans or PA views solely because of late teething alone. However, since we know some serious underlying conditions could cause delayed eruption—such as systemic diseases or syndromes—the best course might involve periodic reassessment coupled with basic consultations to rule out anything more sinister before jumping straight to invasive procedures. Therefore, I maintain my agreement with Option **A**, waiting and watching until around twelve months or so still seems reasonable, though now armed with clearer reasons why delaying action isn't harmful assuming no red flags pop up beforehand prompting urgent investigation instead.
